english: hello / greetings / how to greet someone (formal)
explanation: Universal greeting used in Kodava, Kannada, and most South Indian languages. Use namaskara to greet someone on meeting or parting.
Parting phrase. Literal meaning: may you go well / may you walk auspiciously.
Used to welcome a guest or invite someone in. Informal register.
Standard phrase to invite someone inside. Used when a guest arrives at the door.
The Kodava word for morning. Used in time expressions and implicitly in morning greetings.
Core vocabulary. Used in dative: mane'k (to the house). In compound: adigemane (kitchen).
Past tense of poap'k (to go). Example: naan poanê = I went.
First person singular pronoun in Kodava.
Second person singular pronoun. Informal register.
water in Kodava Takk. niir = water. How do you say water in Kodava? The word for water is niir.
kela = stomach. Used in hunger expressions: kela baip'w (I am hungry), kela baichaind'und (stomach is growling/hungry). Also potte = stomach (alternate word).

Causal connective: 'because'. Attaches to a noun phrase to give a reason for an action. Example: kaal nambale injaang aaspathre'k poochi = went to the hospital because of leg pain. Used to join two clauses causally.

Kodava Takk has no true articles. 'oru' (literally: one) signals indefinite reference, equivalent to 'a' in English. Examples: oru mane = a house; Na'k oru baaLe thaa = Give me a banana. Note: 'ondh' is the counting form of one.

Interrogative adverb: 'why'. Used at the start of a question or embedded in a clause. Examples: ennang thada aachi? = Why are you late? niin ennang banth'le? = Why didn't you come? Also part of the causal phrase: ennang êNchenge = because / the reason why.

2nd person singular past tense question. niin = you, ind = today, enth = what, maaDiyaa = did you do? maaDiyaa is the 2nd person past tense question form (cf. maaDchi = he/she did, 3rd person). Do NOT use maaDchiyaa — that is a wrong blend.

Adjective meaning 'different', 'other', or 'separate'. Example: boare oru paNi = some other work / a different task. When reduplicated as 'boare boare', it intensifies to mean 'various kinds of' or 'all sorts of'. Do not confuse standalone boare (different/other = ಬೇರೆ) with the reduplicated boare boare (various kinds). Equivalent to Kannada ಬೇರೆ.
A warm Kodava greeting conveying goodwill and blessings — used like 'have a good one' or a general well-wish. Not a direct translation of 'thank you'.
Literally 'morning good-wishes'. The standard Kodava morning greeting. polaaka = morning, nallamma = good wishes.
